name: Edgar Allan Poe
description: Haunting lines from the gothic tales and shadow-haunted poems of Edgar Allan Poe, master of mystery and the macabre.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: Once upon a midnight dreary, while I pondered, weak and weary,
quotes.notes: The famous opening line of Poe's best-known poem, setting its sleepless, sorrowful mood.

quotes.content: Quoth the Raven, "Nevermore."
quotes.notes: The poem's haunting refrain, spoken by the ominous bird perched above the chamber door.

quotes.content: Doubting, dreaming dreams no mortal ever dared to dream before;
quotes.notes: The narrator peers into the darkness beyond his door, lost in dread and longing.

quotes.content: And my soul from out that shadow that lies floating on the floor Shall be lifted--nevermore!
quotes.notes: The poem's despairing final lines, sealing the narrator's eternal grief.

quotes.content: Darkness there and nothing more.
quotes.notes: What the narrator finds upon opening the door into the night.

quotes.content: All that we see or seem Is but a dream within a dream.
quotes.notes: Poe's meditation on the elusive boundary between reality and illusion.

quotes.content: I stand amid the roar Of a surf-tormented shore,
quotes.notes: The poem's second stanza, where the speaker confronts time slipping away like sand.

quotes.content: And I hold within my hand Grains of the golden sand--
quotes.notes: An image of life's moments running irretrievably through the fingers.

quotes.content: It was many and many a year ago, In a kingdom by the sea,
quotes.notes: The fairy-tale opening of Poe's final complete poem, a lament for a lost love.

quotes.content: But we loved with a love that was more than love-- I and my Annabel Lee;
quotes.notes: The poem's most quoted profession of a devotion beyond ordinary affection.

quotes.content: I was a child and she was a child, In this kingdom by the sea:
quotes.notes: Recalling the youthful innocence of the speaker and his beloved.

quotes.content: For the moon never beams without bringing me dreams Of the beautiful Annabel Lee;
quotes.notes: The grieving speaker is haunted nightly by visions of his lost love.

quotes.content: And so, all the night-tide, I lie down by the side Of my darling, my darling, my life and my bride,
quotes.notes: The poem's eerie close, the speaker keeping vigil by his beloved's tomb.

quotes.content: Hear the sledges with the bells-- Silver bells! What a world of merriment their melody foretells!
quotes.notes: The bright opening of Poe's onomatopoeic study of bells and the moods they evoke.

quotes.content: Keeping time, time, time, In a sort of Runic rhyme,
quotes.notes: A recurring refrain echoing the insistent rhythm of the ringing bells.

quotes.content: Gaily bedight, A gallant knight, In sunshine and in shadow, Had journeyed long, Singing a song, In search of Eldorado.
quotes.notes: The opening stanza of Poe's ballad about a lifelong, unattainable quest.

quotes.content: "Over the Mountains Of the Moon, Down the Valley of the Shadow, Ride, boldly ride," The shade replied, "If you seek for Eldorado!"
quotes.notes: The pilgrim shade's answer, sending the aged knight ever onward toward his dream.

quotes.content: Helen, thy beauty is to me Like those Nicean barks of yore,
quotes.notes: The opening of Poe's classical tribute, likening a woman's beauty to a homeward voyage.

quotes.content: To the glory that was Greece, To the grandeur that was Rome.
quotes.notes: The poem's celebrated closing image of classical antiquity's splendor.

quotes.content: True!--nervous--very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that I am mad?
quotes.notes: The unsettling opening line of the murderer-narrator protesting his sanity.

quotes.content: It is the beating of his hideous heart!
quotes.notes: The narrator's final confession, undone by the phantom heartbeat of his victim.

quotes.content: I heard all things in the heaven and in the earth. I heard many things in hell.
quotes.notes: The narrator's claim of preternaturally sharpened senses as proof of his soundness.

quotes.content: And Darkness and Decay and the Red Death held illimitable dominion over all.
quotes.source: The Masque of the Red Death (1842)
quotes.notes: The tale's chilling final sentence, as the plague claims even Prince Prospero's revelers.

quotes.content: The "Red Death" had long devastated the country.
quotes.source: The Masque of the Red Death (1842)
quotes.notes: The opening sentence introducing the pestilence no walls can keep out.

quotes.content: During the whole of a dull, dark, and soundless day in the autumn of the year, when the clouds hung oppressively low in the heavens, I had been passing alone, on horseback, through a singularly dreary tract of country;
quotes.source: The Fall of the House of Usher (1839)
quotes.notes: The atmospheric opening that establishes the tale's oppressive gothic dread.

quotes.content: The thousand injuries of Fortunato I had borne as I best could, but when he ventured upon insult I vowed revenge.
quotes.notes: Montresor's opening declaration of the grudge that drives his terrible vengeance.

quotes.content: In pace requiescat!
quotes.notes: Montresor's Latin closing words--"May he rest in peace"--over his walled-up victim.

quotes.content: I was sick--sick unto death with that long agony;
quotes.notes: The opening of the narrator's account of his ordeal before the Inquisition.

quotes.content: Let me call myself, for the present, William Wilson.
quotes.notes: The narrator's evasive opening in Poe's tale of a man pursued by his double.

quotes.content: The death, then, of a beautiful woman is, unquestionably, the most poetical topic in the world.
quotes.notes: Poe's much-debated claim from his essay on how he composed The Raven.

quotes.content: I would define, in brief, the Poetry of words as The Rhythmical Creation of Beauty.
quotes.notes: Poe's signature definition of poetry from his posthumously published essay.
